rows.Name: Thyroid
rows.Function: Butterfly gland in the neck that regulates metabolism (via secreting T3 and T4), associated with iodine
rows.Notes: Pituitary releases TSH -> stimulates Thyroid to produce T3 and T4 -> regulate metabolism with negative feedback to pituitary

rows.Name: Goiter
rows.Description: Enlargement of the thyroid or irregular cell growth in its nodules
rows.Diagnostics: TSH and T4 levels are measured to determine associated hypo/hyperthyroidism
rows.Clinical Manifestations: May be associated with no change in thyroid function or increase/decrease in thyroid hormones

rows.Name: Hyperthyroidism / Graves disease
rows.Description: Excessive secretion of T3 and T4
Pathophysiology: Thyrotropin Receptor Antibody TRAb mimics TSH (leads to over active thyroid secretion, eventually thyrotoxicosis)
Diagnostics: TSH - low, Free T4 and T3 -high
Thyroid Stimulating Immunoglobulin TSI positive if Graves
Radioactive Iodine Uptake test RAIU - increased in graves; focal uptake in toxic nodules
Thyroid ultrasound - enlarged gland or nodules
Clinical Manifestations: Fragile nails, hair loss, moist skin
Thyroid bruit (bloodflow sound at the thyroid)
Management: Methimazole - inhibits TPO - decreases T3 and T4 synthesis, first line therapy unless pregnant. (lower hepatotoxicity than PTU) (inhibits TPO)
Propylthiouracil - inhibits TPO and inhibits peripheral conversion of T4 to T3, preferred in pregnancy or thyroid storm
Reduce stress, avoid caffeine, quit smoking
Maintain high calorie high protein diet
Treatment: Radioactive Iodine - destroys thyroid tissue over time, definitive treatment if non pregnant especially for Graves

Thyroidectomy - immediate reduction, preferred if large goiter, suspicion of cancer, or rapid treatment needed. Substomal removal preferred so no hypothyroidism Preop: lugols solution (decreases thyroid bloodflow); post op: respiratory complications (laryngeal stridor), tetany (have CaCl, Ca gluconate on hand)

rows.Name: Hypothyroidism
Description: Insufficient thyroid hormones, metabolism slows down
rows.Description (primary): Thyroid itself (doesn’t respond to TSH); TSH high, T3 T4 low; causes: autoimmune disease (hashimotos), iodine deficiency, thyroid surgery
rows.Description (secondary): Hypothalamus or Pituitary fails to properly stimulate; TSH T3 T4 all low; causes: pituitary tumors, hypothalamic disease, brain trauma, radiation
Clinical Manifestations: Brain fog, depression, anxiety, dull blank expression
rows.Treatment: Levothyroxine - take at same time every morning on empty stomach, 30-60 min before breakfast. Improvement may take weeks
Nursing: Avoid calcium, iron, antacids, soy products within 4 hours of medication

rows.Name: Hashimoto’s Thyroiditis
rows.Definition: Chronic autoimmune disease that destroys the thyroid gland
rows.Symptoms: Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin
rows.Temperature: Usually normal or mild cold intolerance

rows.Name: Myxedema Coma
rows.Definition: Life-threatening extreme form of severe hypothyroidism
rows.Onset: Sudden deterioration in patients with severe untreated hypothyroidism
rows.Common Trigger: Infection, cold exposure, trauma, surgery, sedatives
rows.Thyroid Hormone Levels: Very ↓ T4, very ↑ TSH (or sometimes normal/low in central cases)
rows.Symptoms: Altered mental status, hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory depression
rows.Treatment: ICU care: IV levothyroxine, hydrocortisone, supportive care

rows.Name: Parathyroid
rows.Function: Main function is to make PTH which controls calcium, phosphorus, magnesium levels in blood
rows.Notes: Smallest organ in the body, 4 glands in neck (behind thyroid)

rows.Name: Hyperparathyroidism
rows.Description (primary): Enlargement of one or more of the glands (causes overproduction of PTH); causes high calcium levels which can cause a variety of health problems
rows.Description (secondary): Chronic low calcium in body, body overcompensates (with increased PTH); common in kidne disease, or after intestinal surgeries or disease
Management: Calcimemetic - drug that mimics calcium in blood (may cause parathyroid to secret less PTH)

rows.Name: Hypoparathyroidism
rows.Description: Uncommon, low levels of PTH leads to low Ca and high phosphorus
rows.Clinical Manifestations: Decreased Ca - tetany, muscle cramps, paresthesia, chvosteks and trousseaus
Management: Oral calcium supplement, Vitamin D, magnesium if low
PTH replacement - very restricted due to side effects
